title.emphasis: III. The ABC Test and Retroactivity

section.para: Since the interactions are service contracts, the ABC Test applies. Crucially, following the California Supreme Court’s decision in Vazquez v. Jan-Pro, the ABC test (established in Dynamex and codified in AB5) applies retroactively to past conduct. Google must prove all three prongs to classify the worker as an independent contractor.

listitem.para: (A) Free from Control: Google exerts significant control by defining strict VRP rules, structuring AI feedback mechanisms, and directing UGC behavior through gamification (e.g., Local Guides).
(B) Outside the Usual Course of Business: This is a major hurdle. Security research, quality assurance, data collection, and AI training are integral to Google's core business operations.

section.para: Prong C requires the worker to be "customarily engaged in an independently established trade, occupation, or business" (Section 5.2). The analysis identifies this as the "single greatest vulnerability" (Section 5.3).

A "hobby" is legally defined as an activity not conducted in a businesslike manner or for profit (Section 5.1). It is "logically impossible" for a hobbyist to satisfy Prong C (Section 5.2). The vast majority of users providing UGC or AI feedback, and many VRP participants, are not operating independent businesses dedicated to these specific services.
Conclusion: Because Google likely fails Prongs B and C, the statutory presumption mandates the classification of these individuals as employees.
